macro_segments.title: Introduction to the Mishna: Redeeming Korbanot with Blemishes and the Principle of Hekdesh Benefit

micro_segments.title: Overview of the Mishna: Difference between Korbanot, Bechor, and Ma'aser regarding redemption and marketplace sale
Gemara's Initial Challenge: How can Hekdesh benefit if the animal is slaughtered in the marketplace before redemption?
Resolution: Hekdesh benefits at the point of redemption, with the marketplace sale happening afterward to raise the estimated value

macro_segments.title: Bechor versus Ma'aser: Why One Can Be Sold and the Other Cannot

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the distinction: Bechor and Ma'aser are not redeemed, so marketplace sale is disrespectful and unnecessary
Gemara's question on Bechor: Why can't it be sold in the marketplace if it has a blemish?
Torah sources: Lo tipadeh for Bechor versus Lo yiga'el for Ma'aser
Resolution for Ma'aser: Rav Sheshes's overnight contemplation and the orphans exception

macro_segments.title: The Ma'aser Behemah of Orphans: Why They May Sell Despite the Biblical Prohibition

micro_segments.title: Rav Idi, Rav Sheshes's attendant, hears the solution and presents it without attribution in the Beit Midrash
Digression on Torah transmission: Rav Yehuda in the name of Rav explains why attribution matters—the teaching continues through the teacher's name
Return to the orphans case: Ma'aser Behemah of orphans can be sold due to concern for loss (Hashavas Aveidah)
The Braisah: Two cases of Ma'aser Behemah—orphans selling normally, or non-orphans using absorption (Havla'ah) into non-meat parts
Debate between Abaye and Rava: Whether orphans can sell outright (Rava) or only via absorption (Abaye)
Proof from Lulav and Etrog: Absorbing cost of Etrog into Lulav price to avoid Shvi'is issues

macro_segments.title: The Biblical vs. Rabbinic Restriction: Why Ma'aser Cannot Be Sold Alive But May Be Sold Dead

micro_segments.title: Rava's proof from the verse on Tmura: Lo yiga'el applies only when the animal is alive, when Tmura is possible
Conclusion: Biblically, selling is prohibited only when alive; Rabbis extended the prohibition after slaughter
Rav Shmuel bar Rav Yitzchak offers an alternate derivation using the verse about blessing after slaughter
Question on absorption (Havla'ah) with bones: Can the cost be absorbed into bones as well, or only into skin and fat?
Rabbi Chiya and Rabbi Shimon dispute whether absorption works for bones; resolved by whether it's sheep (no value) or cow (has value)

macro_segments.title: Deriving the Prohibition Against Selling Ma'aser from Charamim Using Gezerah Shavah

micro_segments.title: The verse sources: Bechor says Lo tipadeh, Ma'aser says Lo yiga'el; Charamim says both Lo yiga'el and Lo yimacher
Rav Chanina's method: Linking Ma'aser and Charamim via Lo yiga'el to derive that Ma'aser cannot be sold
Challenge: Ma'aser could be learned from Bechor via the word Ha'avarah (passing over), so how is Lo yiga'el extra?
Response: The word Lo yiga'el appears by both Ma'aser and Charamim, so we prefer linking them rather than using Ha'avarah from Bechor
Rava's alternative: Lo yiga'el is extra by Charamim (not needed), freeing it to teach about Ma'aser via Rashi's reading (Lo yimacher, not Lo yiga'el)

macro_segments.title: Rav Ashi's Interpretation: Lo Yiga'el Means Lo Yimacher, Proven by Context of Tmura

micro_segments.title: Rav Ashi's direct reading: Lo yiga'el in the context of Tmura (which only works alive) must mean Lo yimacher, not Lo yiga'el redemption
The Gemara's testing: If Lo yiga'el meant redemption, it would apply after slaughter (when it's weaker); that makes no sense—so it must mean sale
The Gemara's final objection and resolution: Why not just write Lo yimacher? Because redeeming needed to be explicitly prohibited; selling is learned by context
